title: How secure is Australia's fuel supply? | The Business | ABC NEWS
description: Australia has enough fuel for now, but there are big questions about our fuel security in the coming months.  Australia imports 90 per cent of the fuel we use.  Most of it comes from Asia, with South Korea, Singapore and India our biggest suppliers of refined fuels, including petrol, diesel and jet fuel.  Most of the crude oil that's still refined in Australia comes from Malaysia and Brunei.  China and South Korea have already started curbing the export of refined fuel products to keep more for their own citizens, and Malaysia says it might as well.  Singapore's foreign minister Vivian Balakrishnan says "the entire global economy has been taken hostage and we will all pay a price for the hostilities."   Energy minister Chris Bowen has said only six fuel shipments to Australia, due after mid-April, have been cancelled or deferred. That's out of an average of 81 per month.  The conflict has taken out about one in five barrels of oil from global supply. The longer that continues the more expensive things get.  Australia can afford to compete, by paying higher prices. while poorer countries have fewer choices.  ANZ senior commodities analyst Daniel Hynes said "Australia has a really strong track record around that, delivering into contracts. And I think that holds our stance within the global energy markets in a fairly high level."  Another point of leverage is liquified natural gas. Australia produces far more than we use and are among the largest exporters in the world.  Some of the countries that send fuel to Australia, need our LNG, so there could be some hard bargains to be driven. #ABCBusiness Subscribe: http://ab.co/1svxLVE Read more here: https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-22/iran-war-leaves-asias-oil-refiners-scrambling/106470932  ABC NEWS provides around the clock coverage of news events as they break in Australia and abroad. title: Cyclone evacuees head home, as Katherine still on flood alert | ABC NEWS
description: 00:00 Evacuated residents from East Arnhem Land communities are starting to return home for the first time since ex-Tropical Cyclone Narelle hit. Remnants from the storm are still effecting the Northern Territory with hundreds without power and thousands more under boil water alerts.  01:41 In Katherine, residents had just returned home after evacuating over the weekend only to be put on alert again with the predictions the river could reach major flood levels by Wednesday. While the town appears to have avoided a second major disaster, many are still on alert, keeping sandbags in place just in case. By Courtney Barrett Peters and Olivana Lathouris for ABC News Northern Territory 05:48 The NT Chief Minister has hit back at opposition claims flood evacuees currently have no control over their lives. title: Hong Kong law gives police new powers to access passwords | The World | ABC NEWS

title: Water crisis looms in Middle East after strikes on desalination plants | The World | ABC News
description: There could be another dark chapter to the war in the Middle East and that's to do with humankind's most basic need -  water. Iran, which has been suffering severe water shortages has accused the United States of striking one of its desalination plants. Tehran is now threatening to strike the Gulf's water infrastructure if the US hits its energy facilities. So is water another frontier in this war?   Kaveh Madani, director of the United Nations University Institute for Water, Environment and Health and the former deputy head of Iran's environment department spoke to The World's Girish Sawlani about the prospect of desalination plants becoming new targets in this conflict.
